jingle: To make a sharp clattering sound; to ring as a little bell, or as small pieces of sonorous metal; as gingling halfpence.
To utter affected or chiming sounds in periods or cadence.
To sound with a fine sharp rattle; to clink; as jingling chains or bells.
To cause to give a sharp sound, as a little bell or as pieces of metal. The bells she jingled, and the whistle blew.
A rattling or clinking sound, as of little bells or pieces of metal.
jurisdiction: Power of governing or legislating. The legislature of one state can exercise no jurisdiction in another.
The power or right of exercising authority. Nations claim exclusive jurisdiction on the sea, to the extent of a marine league from the main land or shore.

The limit within which power may be exercised. Jurisdiction, in its most general sense, is the power to make, declare or apply the law; when confined to the judiciary department, it is what we denominate the judicial power, the right of administering justice through the laws, by the means which the laws have provided for that purpose. Jurisdiction, is limited to place or territory, to persons, or to particular subjects.
[L. jurisdictio; jus, juris, law, and dictio, from and useful to men.
jurisdictional: Pertaining to jurisdiction; as jurisdictional rights.
jurisprudence: The science of law; the knowledge of the laws, customs and rights of men in a state or community, necessary for the due administration of justice. The study of jurisprudence, next to that of theology, is the most important his mind some imperfect account of the origin of the Greeks.]

The science of law; the knowledge of the laws, customs and rights of men in a state or community, necessary for the due administration of justice. The study of jurisprudence, next to that of theology, is the most important and useful to men.
jabber: To talk rapidly or indistinctly; to chatter; to prate.
Rapid talk with indistinct utterance of words.
jabberer: One that talks rapidly, indistinctly or unintelligibly.
jabiru: An aquatic fowl of the crane kind. The Jabiru is the Mycteria Americana. It resembles the stork .
jacamar: A kind of fowls arranged by Linne under the genus Alcedo; but their toes are differently placed, and their food consists of insects. They are about the size of a lark. Numerous species are described. The Jacamars are arranged in a separate genus, Galbula, and along with the woodpeckers in the order of climbers.
jacinth: A species of pellucid gems. Revelation 21:20.
jack: A nickname or diminutive of John, used as a general term of contempt for any saucy of paltry fellow.
The name of an instrument that supplies the place of a boy; an instrument to pull off boots.
An engine to turn a spit; as a kitchen jack; a smoke jack.
jackanapes: A coxcomb; an impertinent fellow. A young upstart jackanapes.
jack-block: A block attached to the top-gallant-tie of a ship, to sway up or to strike the yard.
jackboots: Boots that serve as armor for the legs.
jackdaw: A fowl of the genus Corvus, thievish and mischievous to the farmer.
jackflag: A flag hoisted at the sprit-sail top-mast-head.
jackpudding: A merry Andrew; a buffoon; a zany.
jacksmith: A smith who makes jacks for the chimney.
jackal: An animal of the genus Canis, resembling a dog and a fox; a native of Asia and Africa. It preys on poultry and other small animals. It is the Canis aureus of Linne.
jacket: A short close garment worn by males, extending downwards to the hips; a short coat.
jacobin: The Jacobins, in France, during the late revolution, were a society of violent revolutionists, who held secret meetings in which measures were concerted to direct the proceedings of the National Assembly. Hence, a Jacobin is the member of a club, or other person, who opposes government in a secret and unlawful manner or by violent means; a turbulent demagogue.
jacobine: A monk of the order of Dominicans.
A pigeon with a high tuft.
jacobinic: Resembling the Jacobins of France; turbulent; discontented with government; holding democratic principles.
jacobinical: Resembling the Jacobins of France; turbulent; discontented with government; holding democratic principles.
jacobinism: Jacobinic principles; unreasonable or violent opposition to legitimate government; an attempt to overthrow or change government by secret cabals or irregular means; popular turbulence.
jacobite: A partizan or adherent of James II, king of England, after he abdicated the throne, and of his descendants; of course, an opposer of the revolution in 1688, in favor of William and Mary.
Pertaining to the partizans of James II.
One of a sect of christians in Syria and Mesopotamia, who hold that Jesus Christ had but one nature.
jacobitism: The principles of the partizans of James II.
jacobus: A gold coin, value twenty-five shillings sterling, struck in the reign of James I.
jactitation: A tossing of the body; restlessness.
A term in the canon law for a false pretension to marriage; vain boasting.
jaculation: The action of darting, throwing or lanching, as missive weapons.
jaculator: The shooting fish, a species of Chaetodon.
jaculatory: Darting or throwing out suddenly, or suddenly thrown out; uttered in short sentences.
jade: A mean or poor horse; a tired horse; a worthless nag. Tired as a jade in overloaden cart.
Amean woman; a word of contempt, noting sometimes age, but generally vice. She shines the first of battered jades.
A young woman; in irony or slight contempt.

A mineral called also nephrite or nephritic stone, remarkable for its hardness and tenacity, of a color more or less green, and of a resinous or oily aspect when polished. It is fusible into a glass or enamel. Cleveland divides jade into three subspecies, nephrite, saussurite, and axestone. It is found in detached masses or inhering in rocks.
To tire; to fatigue; to weary with hard service; as, to jade a horse.
jag: A tooth of a saw; a denticulation. In botany, a cleft or division.
jagg: To notch; to cut into notches or teeth like those of a saw.
A tooth of a saw; a denticulation. In botany, a cleft or division.
jagged: Having notches or teeth; cleft; divided; laciniate; as jagged leaves.
jaggedness: The state of being denticulated; unevenness.
jaguar: The American tiger, or once of Brasil, belonging to the genus Felis.
jail: A prison; a building or place for the confinement of persons arrested for debt or for crime, and held in the custody of the sheriff.
A prison; a building or place for the confinement of persons arrested for debt or for crime, and held in the custody Statesand justices of the peace. JUSTICE.
jailbird: A prisoner; one who has been confined in prison.
jailfever: A contagious and fatal fever generated in jails and other places crowded with people.
jakes: A house of office or back-house; a privy.
jalap: The root of a plant, a species of Convolvulus. It is brought in thin transverse slices, and also whole, of an oval shape, hard, solid and heavy. It has little or no taste or smell, but is much used in powder as a cathartic.
jam: A conserve of fruits boiled with sugar and water.
A kind of frock for children.
To press; to crowd; to wedge in.
In England, to tread hard or make firm by treading, as land by cattle.
Among the lead miners of Mendip, a thick bed of stone which hinders them when pursuing the veins of ore.
jamb: Among the lead miners of Mendip, a thick bed of stone which hinders them when pursuing the veins of ore.
In architecture, a supporter; the side-piece or post of a door; the side-piece of a fireplace.
jambee: A name formerly given to a fashionable cane. JAMBEUxXArmor for the legs.
jangle: To quarrel in words; to altercate; to bicker; to wrangle.
To cause to sound untunably or discordantly. --E’er monkish rhymes . Had jangl’d their fantastic chimes.
janizarian: Pertaining to the Janizaries, or their government.
janizary: A soldier of the Turkish foot guards. The Janizaries were a body of infantry, and reputed the grand Seignor’s guards. They became turbulent, and rising in arms against the Sultan, were attacked, defeated and destroyed in Constantinople, in June 1826.
jansenism: The doctrine of Jansen in regard to free will and grace.
jansenist: A follower of Jansen, bishop of Ypres, in Flanders.
jant: To ramble here and there; to make an excursion.
january: The first month of the year, according to the present computation. At the foundation of Rome, March was considered the first month. January and February were introduced by Numa Pompilius‘ .
japan: This name is given to work varnished and figured in the manner practiced by the natives of Japan.
To varnish in the manner of the Japanese.
To black and gloss, as in blacking shoes or boots.
japan-earth: Catechu, a combination of gummy and resinous matter, obtained from the juice of a species of palm tree. Japan-earth or catechu, is obtained by decoction and evaporation from a species of Mimosa. It consists chiefly of tannin combined with a peculiar species of extractive.
japanese: Pertaining to Japan or its inhabitants.
A native of Japan; or the language of the inhabitants.
japanner: One who varnishes in the manner of the Japanese, or one skilled in the art.
japanning: Varnishing in the manner of the Japanese; giving a glossy black surface.
The art of varnishing and drawing figures on wood or other material, in the manner practiced by the Japanese.
japhetic: Pertaining to Japheth, the eldest son of Noah; as the Japhetic nations, which people the North of Asia and all Europe; Japhetic languages.
japu: A bird of Brazil that suspends its nest.
jar: To strike together with a short rattle or tremulous sound; to strike untunably or harshly; to strike discordantly; as a jarring sound. A string may jar in the best master’s hand.
To clash; to interfere; to act in opposition; to be inconsistent. For orders and degrees . Jar not with liberty, but well consist.
To quarrel; to dispute; to clash in words.
To vibrate regularly; to repeat the same sound.
To shake; to cause to tremble; to cause a short tremulous motion in a thing.
jararaca: A species of serpent in America, seldom exceeding 18 inches in length, having prominent veins on its head, and of a dusky brownish color, variegated with red and black spots. It is very poisonous.
javel: To bemire; and as a noun, a wandering or dirty fellow.
jardes: Callous tumors on the legs of a horse, below the bend of the ham on the outside.
jargle: To emit a harsh or shrill sound.
jargon: Confused, unintelligible talk or language; gabble; gibberish; cant. All jargon of the schools.

A mineral, usually of a gray or greenish white color, in small irregular grains, or crystallized in quadrangular prisms surmounted with pyramids, or in octahedrons consisting of double quadrangular prisms.
jarring: Shaking; making a harsh sound; discordant.
jasmin: A plant of the genus Jasminum, bearing beautiful flowers. There are several species. The common white jasmin is a climbing shrub, rising on supports 15 or 20 feet high. The name is also given to several plants of different genera; as the Arabian Jasmin, of the genus Nyctanthes; the bastard Jasmin, of the genus Cestrum, and also of the genus Lycium; the Persian Jasmin, of the genus Syringa; the red Jasmin, of the genus Plumeria; the scarlet and yellow Jasmin, of the genus Bignonia.
jasmine: A plant of the genus Jasminum, bearing beautiful flowers. There are several species. The common white jasmin is a climbing shrub, rising on supports 15 or 20 feet high. The name is also given to several plants of different genera; as the Arabian Jasmin, of the genus Nyctanthes; the bastard Jasmin, of the genus Cestrum, and also of the genus Lycium; the Persian Jasmin, of the genus Syringa; the red Jasmin, of the genus Plumeria; the scarlet and yellow Jasmin, of the genus Bignonia.
jaspachate: A name anciently given to some varieties of agate jasper.
jasper: A mineral of the siliceous kind, and of several varieties. It is less hard than flint or even than common quartz, but gives fire with steel. It is entirely opake, or sometimes feebly translucent at the edges, and it presents almost every variety of color. Its varieties are common jasper, striped jasper, Egyptian jasperIt admits of an elegant polish, and is used for vases, seals, snuff-boxesJasper is a subspecies of rhomboidal quartz, of five kinds, Egyptian, striped, porcelain, common, and agate jasper.
jasperated: Mixed with jasper; containing particles of jasper; as a jasperated agate.
jaspidean: Like jasper; consisting of jasper, or partaking of jasper. JASPONYxThe purest horn-colored onyx, with beautiful green zones, composed of genuine matter of the finest jaspers.
jaundice: j andis. A disease which is characterized by a suffusion of bile over the coats of the eye and the whole surface of the body, by which they are tinged with a yellow color. Hence its name.
jaundiced: j andised. Affected with the jaundice; suffused with a yellow color; as a jaundiced eye.
javelin: A sort of spear about five feet and a half long, the shaft of which was of wood, but pointed with steel; used by horse or foot. Every Roman soldier carried seven javelins.
jaw: The bones of the mouth in which the teeth are fixed. They resemble a horse shoe. In most animals, the under jaw only is movable.
In vulgar language, scolding, wrangling, abusive clamor.
jawed: Denoting the appearance of the jaws.
jawfall: Depression of the jaw; figuratively, depression of spirits.
jazel: A gem of an azure blue color.
jealous: Suspicious; apprehensive of rivalship; uneasy through fear that another has withdrawn or may withdraw from one the affections of a person he loves, or enjoy some good which he desires to obtain; followed by of, and applied both to the object of love and to the rival. We say, a young man is jealous of the woman he loves, or jealous of his rival. A man is jealous of his wife, and the wife of her husband.
Suspicious that we do not enjoy the affection or respect of others, or that another is more loved and respected than ourselves.
Solicitous to defend the honor of; concerned for the character of. I have been very jealous for the Lord God of hosts. 7 Kings 19:70,
jealously: jel’usly. With jealousy or suspicion; emulously; with suspicious fear, vigilance or caution.
jealousness: jel’usness. The state of being jealous; suspicion; suspicious vigilance.
jealousy: That passion of peculiar uneasiness which arises from the fear that a rival may rob us of the affection of one whom we love, or the suspicion that he has already done it; or it is the uneasiness which arises from the fear that another does or will enjoy some advantage which we desire for ourselves. A man’s jealousy is excited by the attentions of a rival to his favorite lady. A woman’s jealousy is roused by her husband’s attentions to another woman. The candidate for office manifests a jealousy of others who seek the same office. The jealousy of a student is awakened by the apprehension that his fellow will bear away the palm of praise. In short, jealousy is awakened by whatever may exalt others, or give them pleasures and advantages which we desire for ourselves. Jealousy is nearly allied to envy, for jealousy, before a good is lost by ourselves, is converted into envy, after it is obta
Suspicious caution or vigilance, an earnest concern or solicitude for the welfare or honor of others. Such was Paul’s godly jealousy for the Corinthians.
Indignation. God’s jealousy signifies his concern for his own character and government, with a holy indignation against those who violate his laws, and offend against his majesty. Psalm 79:5.
jears: In sea-language, an assemblage of tackles by which the lower yards of a ship are hoisted or lowered. Hoisting is called swaying, and lowering is called striking. This word is sometimes written geers or gears.
jeat: A fossil of a fine black color.
jeer: To utter severe, sarcastic reflections; to scoff; to deride; to flout; to make a mock of; as, to jeer at one in sport.
To treat with scoffs or derision.
Railing language; scoff; taunt; biting jest; flout; jibe; mockery; derision; ridicule with scorn. Midas exposed to all their jeers, Had lost his art, and kept his ears.
jeerer: A scoffer; a railer; a scorner; a mocker.
jeeringly: With raillery; scornfully; contemptuously; in mockery.
jeffersonite: A mineral occurring in crystalline masses, of a dark olive green color passing into brown, found imbedded in Franklinite and garnet, in New Jersey.
jehovah: The Scripture name of the Supreme Being. If, as is supposed, this name is from the Hebrew substantive verb, the word denotes the Permanent Being, as the primary sense of the substantive verb in all languages, is to be fixed, to stand, to remain or abide. This is a name peculiarly appropriate to the eternal Spirit, the unchangeable God, who describes himself thus, I am that I am. Exodus 3:14.
jehovist: Among critics, one who maintains that the vowel-points annexed to the word Jehovah in Hebrew, are the proper vowels of the word and express the true pronunciation. The Jehovists are opposed to the Adonists, who hold that the points annexed to the word Jehovah, are the vowels of the word Adonai.
jejune: Dry; barren; wanting interesting matter; as a jejune narrative.
jejuneness: Poverty, barrenness; particularly, want of interesting matter; a deficiency of matter that can engage the attention and gratify the mind; as the jejuneness of style or narrative.
jellied: Brought to the consistence of jelly.
jelly: The inspissated juice of fruit, boiled with sugar.
Something viscous or glutinous; something of the consistency of jelly; a transparent sizy substance, obtained from animal substances by decoction; portable soup.
jellybag: A bag through which jelly is distilled.
jenite: A different orthography of yenite, which see.
jennet: A small Spanish horse, properly genet.
jenny: A machine for spinning, moved by water or steam and used in manufactories.
jentling: A fish, the blue chub, found in the Danube.
jeofail: jef’fail. An oversight in pleading or other proceeding at law; or the acknowledgment of a mistake.
jeopard: jep’ard. To hazard; to put in danger; to expose to loss or injury. Zebulon and Naphtali were a people that jeoparded their lives to the death in the high places of the field. Judges 5:18.
jeoparder: jep’arder. One who puts to hazard.
jeopardize: jep’ardize. To expose to loss or injury; to jeopard.
jeopardous: jep’ardous. Exposed to danger; perilous; hazardous.
jeopardy: jep’ardy. Exposure to death, loss or injury; hazard; danger; peril. They were filled with water and were in jeopardy. Luke 8:23.
jerboa: A quadruped having very short fore legs.
jerk: To thrust out; to thrust with a sudden effort; to give a sudden pull, twitch, thrust or push, as, to jerk one under the ribs; to jerk one with the elbow.

To throw with a quick, smart motion; as, to jerk a stone. We apply this word to express the mode of throwing to a little distance by drawing the arm back of the body, and thrusting it forward against the side or hip, which stops the arm suddenly.
A short sudden thrust, push or twitch; a striking against something with a short quick motion; as a jerk of the elbow. His jade gave him a jerk,
jerkin: A jacket; a short coat; a close waistcoat.
jersey: The finest of wool separated from the rest; combed wool.
jerusalem artichoke: A plant, a species of Helianthus or Sunflower.
jess: Short straps of leather tied round the legs of a hawk, by which she is held on the fist.
A ribbon that hangs down from a garland or crown in falconry.
jessamin: A genus of plants and their flowers. Allow of any mean in poesy. MEAN.
Intervening time; interval of time; interim; meantime.
jesse: A large brass candlestick branched into many sconces, hanging down in the middle of a church or choir.
jessed: Having jesses on; a term in heraldry.
jest: A joke; something ludicrous uttered and meant only to excite laughter. Religion should never be the subject of jest.

The object of laughter or sport; a laughing stock. Then let me be your jest, I deserve it. In jest, for mere sport or diversion; not in truth and reality; not in earnest. --And given in earnest what I begged in jest.
To divert or make merry by words or actions; to joke. Jest not with a rude man, lest thy ancestors be disgraced.
jester: A person given to jesting, sportive talk and merry pranks. --He rambled up and down . With shallow jesters.
jesting: Joking; talking for diversion or merriment.
A joking; concise wit; wit that consists in a trope or verbal figure, in a metaphorical sense of words, or in a double sense of the same word, or in similitude of sound in different words.
jestingly: In a jocose manner; not in earnest.
jesting-stock: A laughing stock; a butt of ridicule.
jesuit: s as z. One of the society of Jesus, so called, founded by Ignatius Loyola; a society remarkable for their cunning in propagating their principles.
jesuited: Conforming to the principles of the Jesuits.
jesuitic: Pertaining to the Jesuits or their principles and arts.
jesuitical: Pertaining to the Jesuits or their principles and arts.
jesuitism: The arts, principles and practices of the Jesuits.
Cunning, deceit; hypocrisy; prevarication; deceptive practices to effect a purpose.
jet: A solid, dry, black, inflammable fossil substance, harder than asphalt, susceptible of a good polish, and glossy in its fracture, which is conchoidal or undulating. It is found not in strata or continued masses, but in unconnected heaps. It is wrought into toys, buttons, mourning jewelsJet is regarded as a variety of lignite, or coal originating in wood.
A spout, spouting or shooting of water; a jet d’eau.
To shoot forward; to shoot out; to project; to jut; to intrude.
To strut; to throw or toss the body in haughtiness.
jetteau: jet’to. A throw or spout of water.
jetsam: In law and commerce, properly, the throwing of goods overboard in order to lighten a ship in a tempest for her preservation. The word may however be used for the goods thus thrown away, or adverbially. Jetsam is where goods are cast into the sea, and there sink and remain under water; flotsam, is where they continue swimming; ligan is where they are sunk in the sea, but tied to a cork or buoy.
jetson: In law and commerce, properly, the throwing of goods overboard in order to lighten a ship in a tempest for her preservation. The word may however be used for the goods thus thrown away, or adverbially. Jetsam is where goods are cast into the sea, and there sink and remain under water; flotsam, is where they continue swimming; ligan is where they are sunk in the sea, but tied to a cork or buoy.
jettison: In law and commerce, properly, the throwing of goods overboard in order to lighten a ship in a tempest for her preservation. The word may however be used for the goods thus thrown away, or adverbially. Jetsam is where goods are cast into the sea, and there sink and remain under water; flotsam, is where they continue swimming; ligan is where they are sunk in the sea, but tied to a cork or buoy.
jetty: A small pier or projection into a river for narrowing it and raising the water above that place.
Made of jet, or black as jet.
jettyhead: The projecting part of a wharf; the front of a wharf whose side forms one of the cheeks of a dock.
jewel: An ornament worn by ladies, usually consisting of a precious stone, or set with one or more; a pendant worn in the ear.
A name expressive of fondness. A mother calls her child, her jewel.
To dress or adorn with jewels.
jewel-house: The place where the royal ornaments are reposited.
jewel-office: The place where the royal ornaments are reposited.
jeweler: One who makes or deals in jewels and other ornaments.
jewish: Pertaining to the Jews or Hebrews. Titus 1:14.
jewishly: In the manner of the Jews.
jewry: Judea; also, a district inhabited by Jews, whence the name of a street in London.
jews-ear: The name of a species of Fungus, the Peziza auricula, bearing some resemblance to the human ear.
jews-frankincense: A plant, a species of Styrax.
jews-harp: An instrument of music shaped like a harp, which, placed between the teeth and by means of a spring struck by the finger, gives a sound which is modulated by the breath into soft melody. It is called also Jews-trump.
jews-stone: The clavated spine of a very large egg-shaped sea urchin petrified. It is a regular figure, oblong and rounded, about three quarters of an inch in length, and half an inch in diameter. Its color is a pale dusky gray, with a tinge of dusky red.
jib: The foremost sail of a ship, being a large stay-sail extended from the outer end of the jib-boom towards the fore-top-mast-head. In sloops, it is on the bow-sprit, and extends towards the lower mast-head.
jib-boom: A spar which is run out from the extremity of the bowsprit, and which serves as a continuation of it. Beyond this is sometimes extended the flying-jib-boom.
jiboya: An American serpent of the largest kind.
jig: A kind of light dance, or a tune or air.
jigger: In sea-language, a machine consisting of a rope about five feet long, with a block at one end and a sheave at the other, used to hold on the cable when it is heaved into the ship, by the revolution of the windlass.
jigmaker: One who makes or plays jigs.
jigpin: A pin used by miners to hold the turn-beams, and prevent them from turning.
jilt: A woman who gives her lover hopes and capriciously disappoints him; a woman who trifles with her lover.
Aname of contempt for a woman.
To encourage a lover and then frustrate his hopes; to trick in love; to give hopes to a lover and then reject him.
To play the jilt; to practice deception in love and discard lovers.
jingling: Giving a sharp fine rattling sound, as a little bell or as pieces of metal.
jippo: A waistcoat or kind of stays for females.
job: A piece of work; any thing to be done, whether of more or less importance. The carpenter or mason undertakes to build a house by the job. The erection of Westminster bridge was a heavy job; and it was a great job to erect Central wharf, in Boston. The mechanic has many small jobs on hand.
A lucrative business; an undertaking with a view to profit. No cheek is known to blush nor heart to throb, Save when they lose a question or a job.
A sudden stab with a pointed instrument. To do the job for one, to kill him.
To strike or stab with a sharp instrument.
jobber: A dealer in the public stocks or funds; usually called a stock- jobber.
One who engages in a low, lucrative affair.
jockey: Aman that rides horses in a race.
A dealer in horses; one who makes it his business to buy and sell horses for gain. Hence,
A cheat; one who deceives or takes undue advantage in trade.
To cheat; to trick; to deceive in trade.
To jostle by riding against one.
jockeyship: The art or practice of riding horses.
jocose: Given to jokes and jesting; merry; waggish; used of persons.
Containing a joke; sportive; merry; as jocose or comical airs.
jocosely: In jest; for sport or game; waggishly.
jocoseness: The quality of being jocose; waggery; merriment.
jocular: Jocose; waggish; merry; given to jesting; used of persons.
Containing jokes; sportive; not serious; as a jocular expression or style- .
jocularly: In jest; for sport or mirth.
joculator: A jester; a droll; a minstrel.
jocund: Merry; gay; airy; lively; sportive. Rural sports and jocund strains.
jog: To push or shake with the elbow or hand; to give notice or excite attention by a slight push. Sudden I jogged Ulysses.
To move by jogs or small shocks, like those of a slow trot. So hung his destiny, never to rot, While he might still jog on, and keep his trot.
To walk or travel idly, heavily or slowly. Thus they jog on, still tricking, never thriving.
A push; a slight shake; a shake or push intended to give notice or awaken attention. When your friend falls asleep at church, give him a jog.
jogger: One who walks or moves heavily and slowly.
One who gives a sudden push.
joggle: To shake slightly; to give a sudden but slight push.
johannes: A Portuguese gold coin of the value of eight dollars; contracted often into joe; as a joe, or half-joe. It is named from the figure of king John, which it bears.
johnapple: A sort of apple, good for spring use, when other fruit is spent.
join: To set or bring one thing in contiguity with another. Woe to them that join house to house, that lay field to field. IsaIah 5:8.
To couple; to connect; to combine; as, to join ideas.

To unite in league or marriage. Now Jehoshaphat had riches and honor in abundance, and joined affinity with Ahab. 2 Chronicles 18:1. What God hath joined together, let not man put asunder. Matthew 19:6.
To associate.& Go near and join thyself to this chariot. Acts 8:29.
To unite in any act. Thy tuneful voice with numbers join.
joinder: A joining; as a joinder in demurrer.
joined: Added; united; set or fastened together; associated; confederated.
joiner: One whose occupation is to construct things by joining pieces of wood; but appropriately and usually, a mechanic who does the wood-work in the covering and finishing of buildings. This is the true and original sense of the word in Great Britain and in New England. This person is called in New York, a carpenter.
joinery: The art of fitting and joining pieces of timber in the construction of utensils or parts of a building, so as to form one entire piece.
joinhand: Writing in which letters are joined in words; as distinguished from writing in single letters.
joint: The joining of two or more things.
In anatomy, the joining of two or more bones; an articulation; as the elbow, the knee, or the knuckle.
A knot; the union of two parts of a plant; or the space between two joints; an internode; as the joint of a cane, or of a stalk of maiz.
A hinge; a juncture of parts which admits of motion.
The place where two pieces of timber are united.
jointed: Formed with articulations, as the stem of a plant.
jointer: A long plane, a joiner’s utensil.
joint-heir: A heir having a joint interest with another. Romans 8:17.
jointly: Together; unitedly; in concert; with cooperation.
With union of interest; as, to be jointly concerned in a voyage.
jointress: A woman who has a jointure.
jointstool: A stool consisting of parts inserted in each other.
joint-tenancy: A tenure of estate by unity of interest, title, time and possession.
joint-tenant: One who holds an estate by joint-tenancy.
jointure: An estate in lands or tenements, settled on a woman in consideration of marriage, and which she is to enjoy after her husband’s decease.
joist: A small piece of timber, such as is framed into the girders and summers of a building to support a floor.
To fit in joists; to lay joists.
joke: A jest; something said for the sake of exciting a laugh; something witty or sportive; raillery. A jealous person will rarely bear a joke.
An illusion; something not real, or to no purpose. Inclose whole downs in walls, ‘tis all a joke! In joke, in jest; for the sake of raising a laugh; not in earnest.
To jest; to be merry in words or actions.
To rally; to cast jokes at; to make merry with.
jole: The cheek; used in the phrase, cheek by jole, that is, with the cheeks together, close, tete a tete.
To strike the head against any thing; to clash with violence.
jollily: With noisy mirth; with a disposition to noisy mirth.
jolliness: Noisy mirth; gayety; merriment; festivity. All was now turned to jollity and game.
Elevation of spirit; gayety. He with a proud jollity commanded him to leave that quarrel for him who was only worthy to enter into it.
jollity: Noisy mirth; gayety; merriment; festivity. All was now turned to jollity and game.
Elevation of spirit; gayety. He with a proud jollity commanded him to leave that quarrel for him who was only worthy to enter into it.
jolly: Merry; gay; lively; full of life and mirth; jovial. It expresses more life and noise than cheerful; as a jolly troop of huntsmen.
Expressing mirth or inspiring it. And with his jolly pipe delights the groves. The coachman is swelled into jolly dimensions by frequent potations of malt liquors.
Exciting mirth and gayety; as jolly May.
Like one in high health; pretty: .
jolly-boat: A small boat belonging to a ship.
jolt: To shake with short abrupt risings and fallings; as a carriage moving on rough ground. The carriage jolts.
To shake with sudden jerks, as in a carriage on rough ground, or on a high trotting horse; as the horse or carriage jolts the rider.
A shock or shake by a sudden jerk, as in a carriage.
jolthead: A greathead; a dunce; a blockhead.
jonquil: A plant of the genus Narcissus or daffodil, bearing beautiful flowers, of various colors, yellow and white.
joso: A small fish of the gudgeon kind.
jostle: jos’l. To run against; to push.
jostled: Run against; pushed. We say, a thing is jostled out of its place.
jot: An iota; a point; a tittle; the least quantity assignable. Till heaven and earth pass, one jot or one title shall in no wise pass from the law till all shall be fulfilled. Matthew 5:78. A man may read much, and acquire not a jot of knowledge, or be a jot the wiser.
To set down; to make a memorandum of.
journal: A diary; an account of daily transactions and events; or the book containing such account.
Among merchants, a book in which every particular article or charge is fairly entered from the waste book or blotter.
In navigation, a daily register of the ship’s course and distance, the winds, weather, and other occurrences.

A paper published daily, or other newspaper; also, the title of a book or pamphlet published at stated times, containing an account of inventions, discoveries and improvements in arts and sciences; as the Journal de Savans; the Journal of Science .
journalist: jur’nalist. The writer of a journal or diary.
journalize: jur’nalize. To enter in a journal.
journey: Travel by land to any distance and for any time, indefinitely; as a journey from London to Paris, or to Rome; a journey to visit a brother; a week’s journey; we made two journeys to Philadelphia.
Passage form one place to another; as a long journey from the upper regions.
It may sometimes include a passing by water.
jur’ny. To travel form place to place; to pass from home to a distance. Abram journeyed, going on still towards the south. Genesis 12:9.
journeying: Traveling; passing from place to place.
A traveling or passing from one place to another; as the journeyings of the children of Israel.
journeyman: Strictly, a man hired to work by the day, but in fact, any mechanic who is hired to work for another in his employment, whether by the month, year or other term. It is applied only to mechanics in their own occupations.
journey-work: Work done for hire by a mechanic in his proper occupation.
jove: The name of the Supreme Deity among the Romans .
The planet jupiter. Or ask of yonder argent fields above. Why Jove’s satellites are less than Jove.
The air or atmosphere, or the god of the air. And Jove descends in showers of kindly rain.
jovial: Under the influence of Jupiter, the planet. --The fixed stars astrologically differenced by the planets, and esteemed Martial or Jovial according to the colors whereby they answer these planets.
Gay; merry; airy; joyous; jolly; as a jovial youth; a jovial throng.
Expressive of mirth and hilarity. His odes are some of them panegyrical, others moral, the rest are jovial or bacchanalian.
jovialist: One who lives a jovial life.
jowler: The name of a hunting dog, beagle or other dog.
joy: The passion or emotion excited by the acquisition or expectation of good; that excitement of pleasurable feelings which is caused by success, good fortune, the gratification of desire or some good possessed, or by a rational prospect of possessing what we love or desire; gladness; exultation; exhilaration of spirits. Joy is a delight of the mind, from the consideration of the present or assured approaching possession of a good. Bring heavenly balm to heal my country’s wounds, Joy to my soul and transport to my lay.
Gayety; mirth; festivity. The roofs with joy resound.
Happiness; felicity. Her heavenly form beheld, all wished her joy.
A glorious and triumphant state. --Who for the joy that was set before him, endured the cross. Hebrews 12:2.
The cause of joy or happiness. For ye are our glory and joy. 7 Thessalonians 2:20.
joyful: Full of joy; very glad; exulting. My soul shall be joyful in my God. Isaiah 61:10. Rarely, it has of before the cause of joy. Sad for their loss, but joyful of our life.
joyfully: With joy; gladly. Never did men more joyfully obey.
joyless: Destitute of joy; wanting joy. With downcast eyes the joyless victor sat. Rarely followed by of; as joyless of the grove.
Giving no joy or pleasure. A joyless, dismal, black and sorrowful issue.
joyous: Glad; gay; merry; joyful. Joyous the birds; fresh gales and gentle airs . Whispered it.
Giving joy. They, all as glad as birds of joyous prime-- . It has of, before the cause of joy. And joyous of our conquest early won.
jubilant: Uttering songs of triumph; rejoicing; shouting with joy. While the bright pomp ascended jubilant.
jubilee: Among the Jews, every fiftieth year, being the year following the revolution of seven weeks of years, at which time all the slaves were liberated, and all lands which had been alienated during the whole period, reverted to their former owners. This was a time of great rejoicing. Hence,
A season of great public joy and festivity.
A church solemnity or ceremony celebrated at Rome, in which the pope grants plenary indulgence to sinners, or to as many as visit the churches of St. Peter and St. Paul at Rome .
judaism: The religious doctrines and rites of the Jews, as enjoined in the laws of Moses. Judaism was a temporary dispensation. Vaiey .
Conformity to the Jewish rites and ceremonies.
judaize: To conform to the religious doctrines and rites of the Jews. They--prevailed on the Galatians to judaize so far as to observe the rites of Moses in various instances.
judaizer: One who conforms to the religion of the Jews.
judaizing: Conforming to the doctrines and rites of the Jews.
judas-tree: A plant of the genus Cercis.
juddock: A small snipe, called also Jack-snipe.
judge: A civil officer who is invested with authority to hear and determine causes, civil or criminal, between parties, according to his commission; as the judges of the king’s bench, or of the common pleas; judges of the supreme court, of district courts, or of a county court. The judge of a court of equity is called a chancellor.
The Supreme Being. Shall not the judge of all the earth do right? Genesis 18:25.
One who presides in a court of judicature.

One who has skill to decide on the merits of a question, or on the value of any thing; one who can discern truth and propriety. A man who is no judge of law, may be a good judge of poetry or eloquence, or of the merits of a painting.

In the history of Israel, a chief magistrate, with civil and military powers. The Israelites were governed by judges more than three hundred years, and the history of their transactions is called the book of Judges.
judged: Heard and determined; tried judicially; sentenced; censured; doomed.
judger: One who judges or passes sentence.
judgeship: judj’ship. The office of a judge.
judging: Hearing and determining; forming an opinion; dooming.
judgment: The act of judging; the act or process of the mind in comparing its ideas, to find their agreement or disagreement, and to ascertain truth; or the process of examining facts and arguments, to ascertain propriety and justice; or the process of examining the relations between one proposition and another.

The faculty of the mind by which man is enabled to compare ideas and ascertain the relations of terms and propositions; as a man of clear judgment or sound judgment. The judgment may be biased by prejudice. Judgment supplies the want of certain knowledge.

The determination of the mind, formed from comparing the relations of ideas, or the comparison of facts and arguments. In the formation of our judgments, we should be careful to weigh and compare all the facts connected with the subject.

In law, the sentence of doom pronounced in any cause, civil or criminal, by the judge or court by which it is tried. Judgment may be rendered on demurrer, on a verdict, on a confession or default, or on a non-suit. Judgment, though pronounced by the judge or court, is properly the determination or sentence of the law. A pardon may be pleaded in arrest of judgment.
The right or power of passing sentence.
judgment-day: The last day, or day when final judgment will be pronounced on the subjects of God’s moral government.
judgment-hall: The hall where courts are held.
judgment-seat: The seat or bench on which judges sit in court.
A court; a tribunal. We shall all stand before the judgment-seat of Christ. Romans 14:10.
judicatory: A court of justice; a tribunal.
judicature: The power of distributing justice by legal trial and determination. A court of judicature is a court invested with powers to administer justice between man and man.
A court of justice; a judicatory.
judicial: Pertaining to courts of justice; as judicial power.
Practiced in the distribution of justice; as judicial proceedings.
Proceeding from a court of justice; as a judicial determination.
Issued by a court under its seal; as a judicial writ.
Inflicted, as a penalty or in judgment; as judicial hardness of heart; a judicial punishment.
judicially: In the forms of legal justice; as a sentence judicially declared.
By way of penalty or judgment; as, to be judicially punished.
judiciary: Pertaining to the courts of judicature or legal tribunals.

That branch of government which is concerned in the trial and determination of controversies between parties, and of criminal prosecutions; the system of courts of justice in a government. An independent judiciary is the firmest bulwark of freedom.
judicious: According to sound judgment; wise; prudent; rational; adapted to obtain a good end by the best means; used of things. Nothing is more important to success in the world than a judicious application of time, unless it may be a judicious expenditure of money.
Acting according to sound judgment; possessing sound judgment; wise; directed by reason and wisdom; used of persons; as a judicious magistrate; a judicious historian.
judiciously: With good judgment; with discretion or wisdom; skillfully. Longinus has judiciously preferred the sublime genius that sometimes errs, to the middling or indifferent one, which makes few faults, but seldom rises to excellence.
judiciousness: The quality of acting or being according to sound judgment.
jug: A vessel, usually earthen, with a swelling belly and narrow mouth, used for holding and conveying liquors.
juggle: To play tricks by slight of hand; to amuse and make sport by tricks, which make a false show of extraordinary powers.
To practice artifice or imposture. Be these juggling fiends no more believed.
To deceive by trick or artifice. Is’t possible that spells of France should juggle . Men into such strange mockeries?
juggler: One who practices or exhibits tricks by slight of hand; one who makes sport by tricks of extraordinary dexterity, by which the spectator is deceived. Jugglers are punishable by law.
A cheat; a deceiver; a trickish fellow.
juggling: Playing tricks by slight of hand; deceiving.
The act or practice of exhibiting tricks of legerdemain.
jugular: Pertaining to the neck or throat; as the jugular vein.
A large vein of the neck.
juice: The sap of vegetables; the fluid part of animal substances.
juse: The sap of vegetables; the fluid part of animal substances.
juiceless: ju’seless. Destitute of juice; dry; without moisture.
juiciness: ju’siness. The state of abounding with juice; succulence in plants.
juicy: ju’sy. Abounding with juice; moist; succulent.
jujub: The name of a plant and of its fruit, which is pulpy and resembles a small plum. The plant is arranged under the genus Rhamnus. The fruit was formerly used in pectoral decoctions, but it is now in little reputation.
jujube: The name of a plant and of its fruit, which is pulpy and resembles a small plum. The plant is arranged under the genus Rhamnus. The fruit was formerly used in pectoral decoctions, but it is now in little reputation.
julep: In pharmacy, a medicine composed of some proper liquor and a sirup of sugar, of extemporaneous preparation, serving as a vehicle to other forms of medicine.
julian: Noting the old account of the year, as regulated by Julius Caesar, which continued to be used till 1752, when the Gregorian year, or new style, was adopted. Julian Alps, called also Carnian, between Venetia and Noricum.
julis: A small fish with a green back.
julus: In botany, a catkin or ament, a species of calyx or inflorescence, consisting of chaffy scales arranged along a stalk, as in hazel, birch, willow.
A genus of multiped insects, of the order of Apters, of a semi- cylindrical form, with moniliform antennae, and two articulated palpi.
july: The seventh month of the year, during which the sun enters the sign Leo. It is so called from Julius, the surname of Caius Caesar, who was born in this month. Before that time, this month was called Quintilis, or the fifth month, according to the old Roman calendar, in which March was the first month of the year.
july-flower: The name of certain species of plants. The clove July-flower is of the genus Dianthus; the queen’s July-flower of the genus Hesperis; and the stock July-flower of the genus Cheiranthus.
jumart: The offspring of a bull and a mare.
jumble: To mix in a confused mass; to put or throw together without order. It is often followed by together. One may observe how apt that is to jumble together passages of Scripture.
To meet, mix or unite in a confused manner.
Confused mixture, mass or collection without order.
jumbled: Mixed or collected in a confused mass.
jumbler: One who mixes things in confusion.
jumbling: Putting or mixing in a confused mass.
jump: To leap; to skip; to spring. Applied to men, it signifies to spring upwards or forwards with both feet, in distinction from hop, which signifies to spring with one foot. A man jumps over a ditch; a beast jumps over a fence. A man jumps upon a horse; a goat jumps from rock to rock.
To spring over any thing; to pass to at a leap. Here, upon this bank and shelve of time, We’d jump the life to come. We see a little, presume a great deal, and so jump to the conclusion.
To bound; to pass from object to object; to jolt. The noise of the rattling of the wheels, and of the prancing horses, and of the jumping chariots. Nahum 3:2.
To agree; to tally; to coincide. In some sort it jumps with my humor.
To pass by a leap; to pass over eagerly or hastily; as, to jump a stream.
juncate: A cheese-cake; a kind of sweetmeat of curds and sugar.
junction: The act or operation of joining; as the junction of two armies or detachments.
The place or point of union.
juncture: A joining; union; amity; as the juncture of hearts.
A union of two bodies; a seam; particularly, a joint or articulation.
The line or point at which two bodies are joined.
A point of time; particularly, a point rendered critical or important by a concurrence of circumstances.
june: The sixth month of the year, when the sun enters the sign Cancer.
jungle: In Hindoostan, a thick wood of small trees or shrubs.
jungly: Consisting of jungles; abounding with jungles.
junior: Younger; not as old as another; as a junior partner in a company. It is applied to distinguish the younger of two persons bearing the same name in one family or town, and opposed to elder; as John Doe junior.
A person younger than another. The fools, my juniors by a year-- .
juniper: A tree or shrub bearing berries of a bluish color, of a warm, pungent, sweet taste, yielding when fresh, by expression, a rich, sweet, aromatic juice. They are useful carminatives and stomachics. The wood of the tree is of a reddish color, hard and durable, and is used in cabinet work and veneering. The oil of juniper mixed with that of nuts makes an excellent varnish; and the resin powdered is used under the name of pounce.
junk: Pieces of old cable or old cordage, used for making points, gaskets, mats, and when untwisted and picked to pieces, it forms oakum for filling the seams of ships.
A small ship used in China; a Chinese vessel.
junket: To feast in secret; to make an entertainment by stealth.
To feast. Job’s children junketed and feasted together often.
junto: Primarily, a select council or assembly, which deliberates in secret on any affair of government. In a good sense, it is not used in English; but hence,
A cabal; a meeting or collection of men combined for secret deliberation and intrigue for party purposes; a faction; as a junto of ministers.
jupiter: The supreme deity among the Greeks and Romans .

One of the superior planets, remarkable for its brightness. Its diameter is about eighty-nine thousand miles; its distance from the sun, four hundred and ninety millions of miles, and its revolution round the sun a little less than twelve years.
jurat: In England, a magistrate in some corporations; an alderman, or an assistant to a bailiff.
juratory: Comprising an oath; as juratory caution.
juridical: Acting in the distribution of justice; pertaining to a judge.
Used in courts of law or tribunals of justice.
juridically: According to forms of law, or proceedings in tribunals of justice; with legal authority.
jurisconsult: Among the Romans, a man learned in the law; a counselor at law; a master of Roman jurisprudence, who was consulted on the interpretation of the laws.
jurist: Aman who professes the science of law; one versed in the law, or more particularly, in the civil law; a civilian.
One versed in the law of nations, or who writes on the subject.
juror: One that serves on a jury; one sworn to deliver the truth on the evidence given him concerning any matter in question or on trial.
jury: A number of freeholders, selected in the manner prescribed by law, empaneled and sworn to inquire into and try any matter of fact, and to declare the truth on the evidence given them in the case. Grand juries consist usually of twenty four freeholders at least, and are summoned to try matters alleged in indictments. Petty juries, consisting usually of twelve men, attend courts to try matters of fact in civil causes, and to decide both the law and the fact in criminal prosecutions. The decision of a petty jury is called a verdict.
juryman: One who is empaneled on a jury, or who serves as a juror.
jurymast: A mast erected in a ship to supply the place of one carried away in a tempest or an engagementThe most probable origin of the word jury, in this compound, is that proposed by Thomson, from the Fr. jour, day, quasi, joure, temporary, or from L. juvare, to assist.
just: A mock encounter on horseback; a combat for sport or for exercise, in which the combatants pushed K, the eleventh letter of the English Alphabet, is borrowed from the Greeks, being the same character as the Greek kappa, answering to the oriental kaph. It represents a close articulation, formed by pressing the root of the tongue against the upper part of the mouth, with a depression of the lower jaw and opening of the teeth. It is usually denominated a guttural, but is more properly a palatal. Before all the vowels, it has one invariable sound, corresponding with that of c, before a, o, and u, as in keelIn monosyllables, it is used after c, as in crack, check, deck, being necessary to exhibit a correct pronunciation in the derivatives, cracked, checked, decked, cracking, for without it, c, before the vowels e and i, would be sounded like s. K is silent before n, as in know, knifeAs a nume
Regular; orderly; due; suitable. When all . The war shall stand ranged in its just array.
Exactly proportioned; proper. Pleaseth your lordship . To meet his grace, just distance ‘tween our armies?
Full; complete to the common standard. He was a comely personage, a little above just stature.
Full; true; a sense allied to the preceding, or the same. --So that once the skirmish was like to have come to a just battle.
justice: To administer justice. of the sheriff.

The virtue which consists in giving to every one what is his due; practical conformity to the laws and to principles of rectitude in the dealings of men with each other; honesty; integrity in commerce or mutual intercourse. Justice is distributive or commutative. Distributive justice belongs to magistrates or rulers, and consists in distributing to every man that right or equity which the laws and the principles of equity require; or in deciding controversies according to the laws and to principles of equity. Commutative justice consists in fair dealing in trade and mutual intercourse between man and man.

Impartiality; equal distribution of right in expressing opinions; fair representation of facts respecting merit or demerit. In criticisms, narrations, history or discourse, it is a duty to do justice to every man, whether friend or foe.
Equity; agreeableness to right; as, he proved the justice of his claim. This should, in strictness, be justness.
Vindictive retribution; merited punishment. Sooner or later, justice overtakes the criminal.
jasponyx: The purest horn-colored onyx, with beautiful green zones, composed of genuine matter of the finest jaspers.
justiceable: Liable to account in a court of justice.
justiceship: The office or dignity of a justice.
justiciary: One that boasts of the justice of his own act.
justiciar: One that boasts of the justice of his own act.
justifiable: That may be proved to be just; that may be vindicated on principles of law, reason, rectitude or propriety; defensible; vindicable. No breach of law or moral obligation is justifiable. The execution of a malefactor in pursuance of a sentence of court, is justifiable homicide.
justifiableness: The quality of being justifiable; rectitude; possibility of being defended or vindicated.
justifiably: In a manner that admits of vindication or justification; rightly.
justification: The act of justifying; a showing to be just or conformable to law, rectitude or propriety; vindication; defense. The court listened to the evidence and arguments in justification of the prisoner’s conduct. Our disobedience to God’s commands~ admits no justification.
Absolution. Vales I hope, for my brother’s justification, he wrote this but as an essay of my virtue.
In law, the showing of a sufficient reason in court why a defendant did what he is called to answer. Pleas in justification must set forth some special matter.
In theology, remission of sin and absolution from guilt and punishment; or an act of free grace by which God pardons the sinner and accepts him as righteous, on account of the atonement of Christ.
justificative: Justifying; that has power to justify.
justifier: He who pardons and absolves from guilt and punishment. That he might be just, and the justifier of him who believeth in Jesus. Romans 3:26.
One who justifies; one who vindicates, supports or defends.
justify: To prove or show to be just, or conformable to law, right, justice, propriety or duty; to defend or maintain; to vindicate as right. We cannot justify disobedience or ingratitude to our Maker. We cannot justify insult or incivility to our fellow men. Intemperance, lewdness, profaneness and dueling are in no case to be justified.

In theology, to pardon and clear form guilt; to absolve or acquit from guilt and merited punishment, and to accept as righteous on account of the merits of the Savior, or by the application of Christ’s atonement to the offender.
To cause another to appear comparatively righteous, or less guilty than one’s self. Ezekiel 16:51-52.
To judge rightly of. Wisdom is justified by her children. Matthew 17:19.
To accept as just and treat with favor. James 2:21, 24-25.
justle: jus’l. To run against; to encounter; to strike against; to clash. The chariots shall rage in the streets; they shall justle one against another in the broad ways. Nahum 2:4.
jus’l. To push; to drive; to force by rushing against; commonly followed by off or out; as, to justle a thing off the table, or out of its place.
justly: According to truth and facts. His character is justly described.
Honestly; fairly; with integrity; as, to do justly. Micah 6:8.
Properly; accurately; exactly. Their feet assist their hands, and justly beat the ground.
In conformity to law, justice or propriety; by right. The offender is justly condemned. The hero is justly rewarded, applauded or honored.
justness: Conformity to truth; as the justness of a description or representation.
Justice; reasonableness; equity; as the justness of a cause or of a demand.
Accuracy; exactness; as the justness of proportions.
jut: To shoot forward; to project beyond the main body; as the jutting part of a building. A point of land juts into the sea.
jutty: A projection in a building; also, a pier or mole.
jut-window: A window that projects form the line of a building.
juvenile: Young; youthful; as juvenile years or age.
Pertaining or suited to youth; as juvenile sports.
juvenility: Light and careless manner; the manners or customs of youth.
juxtaposition: A placing or being placed in nearness or contiguity; as the parts of a substance or of a composition. The connection of words is sometimes to be- ascertained by juxtaposition. K with lances and swords, man to man, in mock fight; a tilt; one of the exercises at tournaments.

K K, the eleventh letter of the English Alphabet, is borrowed from the Greeks, being the same character as the Greek kappa, answering to the oriental kaph. It represents a close articulation, formed by pressing the root of the tongue against the upper part of the mouth, with a depression of the lower jaw and opening of the teeth. It is usually denominated a guttural, but is more properly a palatal. Before all the vowels, it has one invariable sound, corresponding with that of c, before a, o, and u, as in keelIn monosyllables, it is used after c, as in crack, check, deck, being necessary to exhibit a correct pronunciation in the derivatives, cracked, checked, decked, cracking, for without it, c, before the vowels e and i, would be sounded like s. Formerly, k was added to c, in certain words of Latin origin, as in musick, publickBut in modern practice, k is very properly omitted, being ent
